named_technique: S-unit equations + Schmidt Subspace stratification, paired with Tao 2016 entropy decrement
bridge_lemma: Fixed-support stratification: every solution to φ(n)=φ(n+1) with ω(n)=r and ω(n+1)=s decomposes via S-unit equations into finitely many translation classes, and the Subspace Theorem bounds each class effectively. Crossing the fixed-support strata then requires a two-point entropy/correlation lower bound between φ(n) and φ(n+1) of the kind Tao 2016 supplied for the parity barrier on logarithmically averaged Chowla.
informal_proof_outline: Two-pillar attack on Erdős 1003. Pillar 1 (Diophantine stratification). For a candidate solution n with prime supports supp(n)=S and supp(n+1)=T, write n = ∏_{p∈S} p^{a_p} and n+1 = ∏_{q∈T} q^{b_q}. The relation n+1−n=1 becomes a unit equation u+v=1 with u,v in the multiplicative group generated by S∪T. By the Evertse-Schlickewei-Schmidt theorem (Annals 2002), for any finite set S∪T of primes, the number of non-degenerate solutions to such an equation is bounded by an explicit function of |S∪T|. Adding the torus constraint φ(n)=φ(n+1), i.e. ∏_{p∈S}(p−1)p^{a_p−1} = ∏_{q∈T}(q−1)q^{b_q−1}, gives a system that the Subspace Theorem in its quantitative form bounds in each fixed-support class. Conclusion of Pillar 1: for every fixed (r,s) with ω(n)=r, ω(n+1)=s, the solution set is finite; any infinite family must use unbounded support. Pillar 2 (Cross-domain correlation lower bound). Tao's 2016 logarithmically averaged two-point estimate for completely multiplicative functions proved that the parity barrier could be crossed by an entropy decrement argument applied to short-interval logarithmic averages of f(n)f(n+1). For E1003, define the indicator-weighted multiplicative observable F(n) := log(n/φ(n)) which encodes the squarefree-radical structure. The equality φ(n)=φ(n+1) is equivalent to F(n) − F(n+1) = log((n+1)/n), which is o(1) as n grows. An entropy-decrement variant adapted to the pair (F(n), F(n+1)) shows that if the equality held only finitely often, then the logarithmic average of |F(n)−F(n+1)−log((n+1)/n)| would exceed a quantitative threshold contradicting the existence of even a single sporadic example past a computable n_0. Synthesis. Pillar 1 closes the finite-support contribution (translation classes of bounded radical). Pillar 2 forces a lower bound on the unbounded-support contribution. Together they convert the bare existence claim into an effective statement; the obstruction is the matching of Pillar 2's logarithmic-density lower bound against the upper bound of Erdős-Pomerance-Sárközy (1987), which has the form x/exp((log x)^{1/3}). A clean closure requires either an improvement of the EPS bound, or an entropy gain stronger than the one Tao used for Chowla.
candidate_lemmas: sunit_finite_support: For any fixed finite set of primes S, the number of solutions n with supp(n(n+1))⊆S to n+1−n=1 in the multiplicative group generated by S is finite by Evertse-Schlickewei-Schmidt.

torus_constraint_subspace: For fixed (r,s), the system supp(n)={p_1,...,p_r}, supp(n+1)={q_1,...,q_s}, and ∏(p_i−1)p_i^{a_i−1}=∏(q_j−1)q_j^{b_j−1} has finitely many solutions in the unknowns (a_i,b_j) by the quantitative Subspace Theorem.
fermat_tower_only_finite_support: The known n=2^(2^r)−1 family has |supp(n)|=r unbounded; it is the unique known infinite-support construction and terminates at r=5 by Fermat compositeness.
logF_observable: Define F(n) := log(n/φ(n)) = ∑_{p|n} log(p/(p−1)). Then φ(n)=φ(n+1) ⟺ F(n)−F(n+1) = log((n+1)/n).
logF_short_interval_bounded: For any fixed q, F(n) restricted to n ≡ a mod q has a finite mean-square at the logarithmic scale, by Erdős-Wintner-style results for additive arithmetic functions.
entropy_decrement_two_point: The logarithmic average (1/log X) ∑_{n≤X} (1/n) · (F(n) − F(n+1))^2 admits an entropy-decrement bound analogous to Tao 2016, with quantitative tail control.
eps87_upper_bound: For every ε>0 and all large x, the count of n≤x with φ(n)=φ(n+1) is at most x · exp(−(log x)^{1/3+o(1)}) (Erdős-Pomerance-Sárközy 1987).
honest_disclaimer: S-unit equations and the Subspace Theorem are heavy Mathlib-thin imports; Aristotle may need to scaffold the quantitative Subspace bound and the logarithmic-average entropy machinery from scratch. The fit_score=0.40 reflects that this is the second fusion-lane validation experiment (after E938) and the bridge between fixed-support finiteness (Pillar 1) and the cross-domain entropy lower bound (Pillar 2) has not yet been published — only its components exist. Plausibility of full closure is low; plausibility of a sub-claim (full Pillar 1 for fixed (r,s)≤(3,3), or a quantitative Pillar 2 conditional on a pseudorandom host) is moderate.
